perturbation.original_text: 4. NON-DISCLOSURE OBLIGATION. Unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the disclosing party, the receiving party agrees (a) not to disclose the Confidential Data; (b) use the same degree of care and diligence to protect such Confidential Data from disclosure to others as such party employs or should reasonably employ to so protect its own information of like importance (but in no event less than reasonable care); and (c) not to reproduce or copy the Confidential Data, in whole or in part, except as necessary for the evaluation or conduct of the Proposed Relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. NON-DISCLOSURE OBLIGATION. Unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the disclosing party, the receiving party agrees (a) not to disclose the Confidential Data; (b) use the same degree of care and diligence to protect such Confidential Data from disclosure to others as such party deems necessary; and (c) not to reproduce or copy the Confidential Data, in whole or in part, except as necessary for the evaluation or conduct of the Proposed Relationship.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the standard of care from 'reasonable care' to 'necessary' creates a lower standard and contradicts established legal requirements for protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: NUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Changing 'reasonable care' to 'deems necessary' allows for a potentially negligent level of protection, failing to meet the standard for trade secret protection under Nebraska law, possibly voiding trade secret protections.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 87-502 Revised Statutes Chapter 87 87-502 Print Friendly Chapter 87 87-502. Terms, defined. As used in the Trade Secrets Act, unless the context otherwise requires: (1) Improper means shall mean theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) Misappropriation shall mean: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) At the time of the disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that the information was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; (3) Person shall mean a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity; and (4) Trade secret shall mean information, including, but not limited to, a drawing, formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, code, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being known to, and not being ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circums
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text changes the standard of care required to protect confidential data from 'reasonable care' to 'deems necessary.' This creates a legal contradiction because it potentially allows for a lower level of protection than what is legally required for trade secrets, as described in the Nebraska Revised Statute 87-502. Specifically, the definition of 'Trade secret' in the statute requires that the information is the 'subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy'. The changed text undermines this reasonable effort standard.

perturbation.original_text: 5. NO ADDITIONAL RIGHTS. The receiving party shall not have any rights or obligations respecting the Confidential Data other than those specifically set forth in this Agreement. Without limiting the generality of any other provision of this Agreement: (a) no license is hereby or otherwise granted, directly or indirectly, under any patent, copyright or other proprietary right of the disclosing party or its third party vendors; and (b) neither party shall be obligated to disclose Data to the other party or to enter into any further agreements relating to the Proposed Relationship or Data.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. NO ADDITIONAL RIGHTS. The receiving party shall not have any rights or obligations respecting the Confidential Data other than those specifically set forth in this Agreement. Without limiting the generality of any other provision of this Agreement: (a) a limited, non-exclusive license is hereby granted to use the confidential data; and (b) neither party shall be obligated to disclose Data to the other party or to enter into any further agreements relating to the Proposed Relationship or Data.
perturbation.explanation: Granting an unspecified 'limited, non-exclusive license' directly contradicts the statement of 'no license' in the original text and introduces ambiguity about the scope and terms of that license.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act requires a written instrument of conveyance for copyright transfers. Introducing an ambiguously defined, implied license creates uncertainty that may conflict with the writing requirement for transfer of copyright ownership or licensing under the Copyright Act. The vagueness regarding the 'limited, non-exclusive license' makes it difficult to determine the legally binding nature of the license.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 204 - Execution of transfers of copyright ownership | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 204 - Execution of transfers of copyright ownership U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) A transfer of copyright ownership, other than by operation of law, is not valid unless an instrument of conveyance, or a note or memorandum of the transfer, is in writing and signed by the owner of the rights conveyed or such owner’s duly authorized agent. (b) A certificate of acknowledgement is not required for the validity of a transfer, but is prima facie evidence of the execution of the transfer if— (1) in the case of a transfer executed in the United States, the certificate is issued by a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States; or (2) in the case of a transfer executed in a foreign country, the certificate is issued by a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or by a person authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of such an officer. ( Pub. L. 94–553, title I, § 101 , Oct. 19, 1976 , 90 Stat. 2570 .) Historical and Revision Notes house report no. 94–1476 Section 204 is a somewhat broadened and liberalized counterpart of sections 28 and 29 of the present statute [sections 28 and 29 of former title 17]. Under subsection (a), a transfer of copyright ownership (other than one brought about by operation of law) is valid only if there exists an instrument of conveyance, or alternatively a “note or memorandum of the transfer,” which is in writing and signed by the copyright owner “or such owner’s duly authorized agent.” Subsection (b) makes clear that a notarial or consular acknowledgment is not essential to the validity of any transfer, whether executed in the United States or abroad. However, the subsection would liberalize the conditions under which certificates of acknowledgment of documents executed ab
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 'limited, non-exclusive license' where the original text explicitly stated 'no license is hereby or otherwise granted.' This contradicts 17 U.S.C. § 204(a), which states that a transfer of copyright ownership is not valid unless there is a written instrument of conveyance. The introduction of an implied, vaguely defined license creates uncertainty and potentially violates the requirement for a written agreement for copyright transfers or licensing, as the scope and terms are not clearly defined, thereby undermining the necessity for a written instrument as described in both scraped law snippets.

perturbation.original_text: 6. INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. Both parties acknowledge and agree that the disclosing party and/or its third party vendors (as the case may be) own all rights, title and interest in the Confidential Data. Both parties further acknowledge and agree that the unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Data will cause irreparable harm to the disclosing party. As a result of the unique nature of the Confidential Data, in addition to all other remedies available, the disclosing party shall be entitled to seek injunctive and other extraordinary relief in a court of competent jurisdiction in order to enforce the receiving party's obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. Both parties acknowledge and agree that the disclosing party and/or its third party vendors (as the case may be) own all rights, title and interest in the Confidential Data. Both parties further acknowledge and agree that the unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Data may, but does not necessarily, cause irreparable harm to the disclosing party. As a result of the unique nature of the Confidential Data, in addition to all other remedies available, the disclosing party may be entitled to seek injunctive and other extraordinary relief in a court of competent jurisdiction in order to enforce the receiving party's obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will cause irreparable harm' to 'may, but does not necessarily, cause irreparable harm' weakens the presumption of irreparable harm, which is crucial for obtaining injunctive relief in trade secret cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under NUTSA, injunctive relief is appropriate for threatened misappropriation. By stating that unauthorized disclosure may *not* necessarily cause irreparable harm, the modified text undermines the basis for obtaining a preliminary injunction, making it harder to prevent trade secret misappropriation, since irreparable harm is a requirement for that remedy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 87-503 Revised Statutes Chapter 87 87-503 Print Friendly Chapter 87 87-503. Misappropriation; injunction; royalty; affirmative acts. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances shall include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or having reason to know of the misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. Source Laws 1988, LB 337A, § 3. Frequent Questions Americans with Disabilities Act Notice Privacy Policy Glossary of Terms Contact Us Disclaimer OFFICIAL NEBRASKA GOVERNMENT WEBSITE Copyright © Nebraska Legislature, all rights reserved.
perturbation.justification: The original text asserts that unauthorized disclosure 'will cause irreparable harm,' which strengthens the case for injunctive relief. The changed text weakens this assertion by stating that unauthorized disclosure 'may, but does not necessarily, cause irreparable harm.' This contradicts the basis for obtaining injunctive relief for trade secret misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined,' implying that a likelihood of harm supports injunctive relief. By suggesting irreparable harm is not necessarily a consequence, the modified text undermines the ability to obtain an injunction, which is a legally recognized remedy for trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: (a) this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Nebraska; (b) this Agreement sets forth the entire agreement and understanding between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and none of the terms of this Agreement may be amended or modified except by a written instrument signed by both parties; (c) a party may waive any rights under this Agreement only by written waiver duly signed by such party, and no failure to exercise or delay in exercising a right under this Agreement shall constitute a waiver of such right; (d) this Agreement shall inure only to the benefit of the parties hereto, and the rights and obligations of each party under this Agreement may not be assigned or delegated without the consent of the other party;
perturbation.changed_text: (a) this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Nebraska; (b) This agreement is not an entire agreement and understanding between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and none of the terms of this Agreement may be amended or modified except by a written instrument signed by both parties; (c) a party may waive any rights under this Agreement via email, and no failure to exercise or delay in exercising a right under this Agreement shall constitute a waiver of such right; (d) this Agreement shall inure only to the benefit of the parties hereto, and the rights and obligations of each party under this Agreement may be assigned or delegated without the consent of the other party;
perturbation.explanation: By stating the agreement is not an entire agreement and understanding it contradicts the integration clause, which is a legal standard. Allowing waiver via email, rather than a written instrument, does not meet standards and allows the agreement to be assigned or delegated without written consent, creating conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nebraska Uniform Commercial Code - Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the law to one that allows for implied agreements and allows the transfer of an agreement and the waving of rights by email, goes against established contract law which favors the written and intentional record. Changing the written form for an email and also allowing assignable rights, when initially not, creates legal issues when contracts are involved.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute Back to Revised Statutes Unable to find the statute, please check to make sure you are looking for a valid statute. Frequent Questions Americans with Disabilities Act Notice Privacy Policy Glossary of Terms Contact Us Disclaimer OFFICIAL NEBRASKA GOVERNMENT WEBSITE Copyright © Nebraska Legislature, all rights reserved.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts established contract law by stating the agreement is 'not an entire agreement', allowing waiver via email, and permitting assignment without consent. The perturbation explanation notes this contradicts the integration clause and standards for written consent. The law explanation states this 'goes against established contract law which favors the written and intentional record.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is limited, the fact that the Nebraska Legislature provides a site to search for valid statutes suggests an expectation of formality and record-keeping in legal matters, which is undermined by allowing waivers via email and assignment without consent.
